hint: the single problem this product solves and for whom
why build this now / why it doesn't already exist for them
what makes this better than the obvious alternative or doing nothing
the one person this is for; their context and skill level
the concrete task the user hires this to accomplish
who this is explicitly NOT for, to keep scope honest
the smallest version that is still useful and shippable
tempting features deliberately left out of v1
what 'finished enough to use' looks like concretely
the main things the system stores and their relationships
where canonical data lives and what's derived/cached
sensitive data, where it lives, how long it's kept
the few big parts and what each owns
what's intentionally separated (core vs shared vs disposable)
where state lives and how parts stay consistent
the main happy-path journey from open to value
the empty / first-launch experience before any data
what the user sees when things fail or input is bad
third-party APIs/services depended on and why
how users (if any) are authenticated and authorized
what costs money at scale (API calls, storage, compute)
the most likely ways this breaks in real use
behavior with no network or a dependency down
where data could be lost or corrupted, and the guard
where this runs and how it gets there
how often it ships and how changes reach users
how to undo a bad release safely
the one number that says this is working
what's measured to know that, and how
the bar that means stop polishing and ship
